question: Consider the three-dimensional XY lattice model on a simple cubic lattice with quenched columnar (line) disorder: in-plane couplings J_{i,x} and J_{i,y} are drawn uniformly from {0.1, 1.9} for each site column, while z-couplings are uniform J_{i,z} = 1. The model describes the superconducting transition in a material with parallel columnar defects. An anisotropic finite-size scaling (FSS) analysis is performed with aspect ratio L_z ~ L^{zeta}, where zeta is the anisotropy exponent (ratio of parallel to perpendicular correlation-length exponents). From Monte Carlo FSS using the Binder ratio g = 1 - <M^4>/(3<M^2>^2) and helicity modulus, determine the following exponents: (1) nu: the perpendicular (in-plane) correlation-length exponent,     where xi_perp ~ |T - T_c|^{-nu}. (2) zeta: the anisotropy exponent defined by xi_z ~ xi_perp^{zeta}     (equivalently nu_parallel / nu_perp). (3) z_XY: the dynamic critical exponent for single-spin heat-bath Monte Carlo dynamics     (local phase-variable relaxational updates), defined by tau ~ xi_perp^{z_XY}. (4) z_loop: the dynamic critical exponent for elementary-vortex-loop heat-bath Monte Carlo     dynamics (loop updates of the dual model), defined by tau ~ xi_perp^{z_loop}. Report as a JSON object with keys "nu", "zeta", "z_XY", "z_loop". Each value should be a float.

metadata.system: 3D XY lattice model, simple cubic lattice, quenched columnar disorder (in-plane couplings from {0.1,1.9}, z-couplings=1)
metadata.source_excerpt: correlation length exponent is nu=1.2+/-0.1; anisotropy exponent zeta=1.3+/-0.1; dynamic exponent z_XY=2.6+/-0.1 (single-spin heat-bath); z_loop=3.4+/-0.1 (elementary-loop heat-bath, dual loop model).
metadata.difficulty_note: Columnar disorder gives new universality class; anisotropic FSS required. Both static and dynamic exponents. Two distinct dynamic universality classes.
